title: You Have Achieved Much
narrative: It does not come as a monster. It comes as a thought — warm, reasonable, deserved. You have walked far. You have suffered. The voice is not wrong about the facts. Prelest operates this way: not through lies but through a truth held upside down, with the self at the center.

choices.text: Give thanks and continue walking
Trace where this thought came from
Rest — but on your knees

title: Nothing Matters
narrative: Not a voice but a weight. Everything you have done feels thin, unconvincing, temporary. The dungeon ahead holds nothing worth finding. The fathers called this acedia — not laziness but the soul's refusal to be where it is. Evagrius said it was the most dangerous of the demons.

choices.text: Move your body before your heart follows
Name what you still love, however small
Stay with the weight until it shifts

title: They Will Notice
narrative: You imagine the moment of return — the recognition in their eyes, the stories told about what you did here. Not greed. Not pride, exactly. Only the quiet wish to be seen. Kenodoxia, the fathers wrote, is vainglory: the desire to receive glory that belongs to God. Its root is thin but its branches reach everywhere.

choices.text: Do the next thing as if no one will ever know
Ask whose glory you are seeking
Redirect the wish into action without audience

title: The Injustice Demands a Response
narrative: It was unjust. That much is true. The anger is not wrong in its object — it is right. Thymos, the passionate faculty, was made for exactly this. But the fathers noted that even righteous anger, held too long, alters what it passes through. The fire meant for the enemy has begun to burn the one who carries it.

choices.text: Act once, precisely, then release it
Pray for the one who wronged you
Name what you are protecting, not what you are against

title: Only Comfort
narrative: It is cold and you are tired. What presents itself now is small — not a vice, only a rest, only a warmth, only comfort. Porneia rarely arrives as lust. It arrives as relief. The fathers were not against pleasure; they were against pleasure as a god. The question is not what you want but what you are making of the wanting.

choices.text: Name the want and offer it up
Move before the thought takes root
Ask what is actually hungry here

title: The Bright Messenger
narrative: It came in the form of light — radiant, beautiful, speaking of God's love for you specifically. It confirmed your path, praised your progress, and offered a vision of your destiny. The fathers read this in Paul's second letter to Corinth: Satan transforms himself into an angel of light. The test is simple: does it exalt you or empty you?

choices.text: Reject it without investigation
Test its word against the tradition
Bow to the ground and ask it to depart

title: You Have Tried Enough
narrative: Not the sudden weight this time — the slow erosion. You have fought before. You have tried this path before. It led here. The voice is gentle, genuinely kind-sounding: 'You have given what you had. It is enough now. Stop.' The fathers knew this variant. It comes in the second half of the journey, when the first fervor is spent.

choices.text: Take one step and then one more
Find someone who needs what you still have
Ask whether 'enough' was ever yours to give

title: The Posture of Prayer
narrative: You are about to pray. Something in you adjusts — checks the angle, considers the posture, wonders if it looks right. Not vanity about your body. Kenodoxia is subtler: the need for your piety to have a witness. The Lord addressed this precisely, the fathers noted — not because external prayer is wrong but because the audience changes everything.

choices.text: Turn your face to the wall and pray anyway
Notice the thought and let it pass without feeding it
Pray shorter than you planned

title: The Consuming Fire
narrative: The anger was clean at first — a response to real wrong, proportionate, pointed. But it has been months. You have rehearsed the injury so many times the rehearsal has become a home. The fathers called this the mutation of thymos: righteous passion curdled into a structure you now live inside. It has become easier than peace.

choices.text: Ask what it would cost to stop rehearsing it
Grieve what was lost and let the anger follow
Fast from the rehearsal for one day

title: Without the Cross
narrative: It offers you theosis directly — union with God, bypassing what remains. No more pain. No more descent. Only the ascent. It is not a temptation to sin but a temptation to arrive at the destination by a road that skips the Cross. The fathers knew this as the most sophisticated of all: the offer of the goal without the way.

choices.text: Refuse — the way is the destination
Bow toward what you must walk through
Empty yourself of the offer itself
